evidence_quotes.quote: 87% of games that launched before 2010, are no longer available for customers.

paragraphs.text: Greetings, Like so many others I have growing concerns with the software/hardware industry's lack of accountability in regards to preserving products already purchased. A study from 2023 found that about 87% of games that launched before 2010, are no longer available for customers. A lot have hard copies that they may still be able to install, but digital versions are gone. Even though consumers have paid for them. https://www.tweaktown.com/news/92301/new-study-finds-that-87-percent-of-games-released-before-2010-are-no-longer-available/index.html https://gamehistory.org/study-explainer/ This problem will only get worse, considering that more and more companies happily shut down games when they no longer turn enough of a profit and even if they do. The game, "The Crew" is the most recent "big" example. While there are inherent expenses supporting an infrastructure, this can be worked around quite easily according to many game developers themselves. It is often management that refuse to work "end-of-life" into the development of games/software. In regards to hardware. The most common issue is companies refusal to support aging hardware with operating systems(mobile phones, tablets, TVs etc.) For the most part under the guise of having too many variations of hardware to deliver regular updates to the software that makes the hardware useable and safe. We see this with the recent change where mobile phone companies had to agree to support their devices for at least 7 years. I hope this will gain traction for the consumer agency part of the EU, since this is also an environmental concern because of all the E-waste.
